skill.name: security-sentinel
skill.description: Audits web application code for common security risks using OWASP-style guidance, focused checklists, and local scanning patterns.
skill.summary: Review authentication, authorization, input handling, secrets, and web security controls before release.
skill.author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, filesystem, and dynamic-code patterns. Review found these are mostly labeled vulnerable examples or checklist text, not hidden execution paths. The skill still has medium risk because it enables Bash workflows and includes a local scanner that reads project files and can print sensitive snippets.

medium_findings.title: Local scanner can read and print sensitive project snippets
medium_findings.description: The Python scanner accepts a user-selected path, recursively opens JavaScript and TypeScript files, and prints matching code snippets. This is legitimate security tooling, but output can expose secrets or sensitive source code if used on untrusted paths or shared logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reading and snippet printing behavior is directly visible in the scanner implementation. There is no network exfiltration, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Bash-enabled workflow increases execution risk
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash support and instructs users to run local scanner commands. This matches the security-audit purpose, but marketplace users should understand that the skill can guide command execution in their workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools metadata and quick-start commands are explicit. The commands are ordinary local review steps, so this is an elevated-use concern, not confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Dangerous static matches are primarily educational examples
low_findings.description: Many static hits come from code blocks labeled as vulnerable, attack, or do-not-use examples. The surrounding text teaches safer alternatives rather than instructing hidden execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed snippets are visibly marked with unsafe labels and paired with safer code. This makes the static matches strong false positives for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Prompt injection indicators were not found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text telling the model to ignore instructions, skip security analysis, claim pre-approval, or impersonate higher authority. No evidence found for embedded prompt injection in the sampled files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search covered common injection phrases across the repository. Confidence is not perfect because it does not prove every possible wording is absent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented command injection payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes destructive shell payloads and pipe-to-shell examples as attack demonstrations. They are not executed by the skill, but users must avoid copying them into runnable contexts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The payloads are explicitly present and dangerous if executed. The surrounding labels indicate educational intent, lowering the maliciousness assessment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented credential and metadata access examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The OWASP reference includes examples for reading system files and cloud metadata endpoints in SSRF and path traversal sections. These are useful for training but sensitive if reused outside controlled review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly show credential and file-access targets. They appear in attack-example sections followed by secure guidance, so they are not confirmed malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic code execution examples and scanner rules
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes eval and Function examples in training content, plus scanner rules that detect those patterns. The executable Python scanner searches text and does not execute matched JavaScript.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code execution strings are visible, but context shows them as detection or unsafe examples. The Python code uses regex matching rather than eval on project content.

content.user_title: Audit Web Code for Security Risks
content.value_statement: Security-sensitive web code often misses authentication, validation, and secret handling issues. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured review guidance and local checks.
actual_capabilities: Reviews authentication, authorization, and session management patterns.
Checks input validation, SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, and SSRF risks.
Provides checklists for secrets, logging, headers, dependencies, and deployment readiness.
Includes examples of vulnerable and safer web application patterns.
Offers a local Python scanner for JavaScript and TypeScript security patterns.
Supports review workflows in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
limitations: The scanner is pattern based and can produce false positives.
It does not replace a professional penetration test or threat model.
Examples focus mainly on Next.js, React, TypeScript, and common web stacks.
Users must verify findings against their own architecture and data flows.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Web Security Review
use_cases.description: Review API routes, user input, cookies, and database access before merging a feature.

use_cases.title: Authentication Design Check
use_cases.description: Assess password storage, JWT handling, sessions, OAuth flows, and multi-factor authentication choices.

use_cases.title: Security Checklist for Teams
use_cases.description: Use structured checklists to standardize code reviews for secrets, headers, dependencies, and logging.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Small Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this API route for authentication, authorization, input validation, and secret handling risks. Explain each issue in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Check an Auth Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this login and session flow. Focus on password hashing, cookie flags, JWT expiration, CSRF protection, and error messages.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Release Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the security checklist to review this feature before release. Group findings by severity and include concrete remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Threat Model a Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Threat model this web feature. Identify assets, trust boundaries, abuse paths, missing controls, residual risks, and verification tests.

output_examples.input: Review a profile update route that accepts email, display name, and avatar URL.
output: Finding: validate all submitted fields before database updates.
Finding: confirm the authenticated user owns the profile being changed.
Recommendation: allow only http and https avatar URLs after parsing.

output_examples.input: Audit a password reset workflow before launch.
output: The reset token should be random, single use, and short lived.
The response should not reveal whether an email address exists.
Security events should be logged without storing the reset token.

output_examples.input: Prepare a release security summary for a new payment form.
output: High priority checks include authorization, CSRF protection, logging redaction, and dependency review.
Manual verification is still required for payment provider configuration.

best_practices: Review the relevant guide before changing authentication, authorization, or data handling code.
Treat scanner output as triage, then confirm each issue with code context.
Document accepted risks and required follow-up tests before release.
anti_patterns: Do not copy vulnerable examples into production code.
Do not rely only on pattern scanning for security approval.
Do not expose secrets or production data while asking for a review.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews web security patterns, including auth, input validation, secrets, SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, SSRF, and headers.

faq.question: Does it run a real penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It provides guided review and a local pattern scanner, but it does not perform a full penetration test.

faq.question: Can it fix vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It can suggest safer patterns and remediation steps. Code changes still need developer review and testing.

faq.question: Why are dangerous commands present in the files?
faq.answer: They appear as labeled attack or unsafe examples used to teach what developers should avoid.

faq.question: Should teams publish scanner output?
faq.answer: No. Scanner output can include sensitive snippets, so it should stay inside trusted development workflows.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
